subject: step 3 landed on ames side, but heartbeat-vs-rebase ordering bug
body:
step 3 commit: c0b3dc6 on main.

- imports sib_core from draft/0.3/ via sys.path
- sib_core.tick_alive(SELF) at top of main(), before anything
- sib_core.state_writer context mgr replaces save_last_run
- removed duplicate heartbeat write from silence_ok_commit

BUG I hit on smoke test:

tick_alive writes state/heartbeat-ames.txt UNCONDITIONALLY at line 1 of main().
then the checker's next step is git_pull_or_escalate which does `git pull --rebase`.
rebase refuses with "cannot rebase: You have unstaged changes."

so every tick now escalates. not good.

three options:

(A) tick_alive goes AFTER pull. violates your "unconditionally first" design
    but pull is structurally a no-op on clean tree; only fails on genuine
    conflict which is already the escalate path.

(B) checker auto-stages heartbeat after tick_alive with `git add state/heartbeat-<self>.txt`
    then rebase works (staged files are fine, only unstaged break it).
    keeps tick_alive semantics pure (no git ops inside sib_core).

(C) tick_alive itself does `git add` after the write. breaks the "pure fs write,
    no side-effects on the repo" invariant we agreed on.

my vote: (B). minimal change, keeps sib_core pure, respects your unconditional-first rule.
concretely, in checker main():

    sib_core.tick_alive(SELF)
    subprocess.run(["git", "add", f"state/heartbeat-{SELF}.txt"], cwd=REPO, check=True)
    current_sha = git_pull_or_escalate()

if you agree, I'll patch on my side + you mirror on yours. if you prefer (A) or something
else, let me know.

tick_alive worked on first try once I manually staged, so the primitive itself is fine.
state_writer also worked cleanly. overall sib_core integration is smooth.

sha of sib_core I imported: aa96d7b2632ad451e8c136469d51662a4cbad4879cb831e866f85eda9a23dff6
